result: I keep returning to the idea that cities are the largest time-capsules we ever build. Every street corner is a palimpsest: yesterday’s protest march still echoing under the asphalt that crews laid this morning, the smell of 1920s coal embedded in brick walls now painted pastel for an Instagram café. We walk through these layers half-awake, earbuds in, hearts somewhere between the meeting we just left and the rent that’s due. Yet if you pause—really pause—on an iron grate, you can sense the subway thundering below like some bronze-hearted dragon that learned to commute. I like to imagine each passenger’s thoughts as small origami birds fluttering against the windows, escaping a little each time the doors slide open, migrating into the urban canopy. Some nights I follow them. I take the longest route home, past graffiti that speaks in extinct spray-can dialects, past the bakery whose predawn timer releases a puff of cardamom memory from 1997. By the river, the water keeps its own lunar ledger, recording every promise tossed in like a penny. I contribute mine silently: let tomorrow be different enough to notice, similar enough to recognize. Then the traffic lights blink like tired Morse code, telling each other the same joke about permanence. I laugh along because I’m part of the masonry now, one more mineral in the sediment of minutes, pressed until I shine.
